learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Greedy Algorithms, Loops, Time Complexity, Space Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine a teacher rewarding students based on performance. Every student receives at least one chocolate. If a student's score is higher than the student sitting immediately beside them, they must receive more chocolates. The goal is to distribute the minimum number of chocolates while satisfying these fairness rules.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine a teacher rewarding students based on performance. Every student receives at least one chocolate. If a student's score is higher than the student sitting immediately beside them, they must receive more chocolates. The goal is to distribute the minimum number of chocolates while satisfying these fairness rules.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this Two-Pass Greedy pattern whenever constraints depend on both the left and right neighboring elements.
learning.signals: Whenever constraints depend independently on both left and right neighbors, think of the Two-Pass Greedy pattern.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: There are n children standing in a line. Each child is assigned a rating value given in the integer array ratings. You are giving candies to these children following two rules: (1) Every child must have at least one candy. (2) Children with a higher rating than an immediate neighbor must receive more candies than that neighbor. Return the minimum number of candies you need to distribute.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Repeatedly scan the array and adjust candy counts until all neighbor conditions are satisfied.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: A single left-to-right pass only satisfies the left neighbor rule. A right-to-left pass is also needed to satisfy the right neighbor rule. Taking the maximum candies required from both directions guarantees the minimum valid distribution.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: One traversal cannot satisfy both neighboring constraints. Two greedy passes solve the problem.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever constraints depend independently on both left and right neighbors, think of the Two-Pass Greedy pattern.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: The first pass guarantees every increasing sequence from the left is satisfied. The second pass fixes decreasing sequences from the right without breaking previous assignments. Every child is processed twice, giving the minimum valid distribution.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever constraints depend independently on both left and right neighbors, think of the Two-Pass Greedy pattern.
commonWrongPaths: Overwriting instead of taking the maximum during the second pass.
Forgetting every child must receive at least one candy.

hints: Every child starts with one candy.
Think about satisfying left and right neighbors independently.
commonMisconceptions: Overwriting instead of taking the maximum during the second pass.
Forgetting every child must receive at least one candy.

pattern.name: Two Pass Greedy
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever constraints depend independently on both left and right neighbors, think of the Two-Pass Greedy pattern.

approaches.title: Repeated Adjustments
approaches.idea: Repeatedly scan the array and adjust candy counts until all neighbor conditions are satisfied.
algorithm: Assign one candy to every child.
Increase candies whenever a rule is violated.
Stop when no further updates occur.

code.java: Brute force repeated simulation is omitted because it is inefficient.

approaches.title: Two Auxiliary Arrays
approaches.idea: Compute the minimum candies required from the left side and from the right side separately, then take the maximum for every child.

code.java: Uses two arrays to store left and right requirements.

approaches.title: Two Pass Greedy
approaches.idea: Initialize every child with one candy. Traverse from left to right to satisfy the left neighbor condition. Then traverse from right to left to satisfy the right neighbor condition by taking the maximum candy count required from either direction.
approaches.intuition: The first pass guarantees every increasing sequence from the left is satisfied. The second pass fixes decreasing sequences from the right without breaking previous assignments. Every child is processed twice, giving the minimum valid distribution.
algorithm: Initialize candy array with 1 for every child.
If current rating > previous rating, assign previous candies + 1.
If current rating > next rating, update current candies using max(current, next + 1).
approaches.dryRun:
ratings=[1,0,2]
Initial=[1,1,1]
Left Pass=[1,1,2]
Right Pass=[2,1,2]
Total=5

visualization.description: Use one pass to satisfy the left neighbor and another pass to satisfy the right neighbor.

interviewFollowUps: Can this be solved using O(1) extra space?
What happens when ratings are equal?
Can the problem be solved in one traversal?
How would you prove the greedy strategy?
interviewTips: Start with every child receiving one candy.
Explain why one pass is insufficient.
Mention taking the maximum during the second pass.
Discuss the O(1) space optimization as a follow-up.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: One traversal cannot satisfy both neighboring constraints. Two greedy passes solve the problem.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Initialize → Left Pass → Right Pass → Sum.

importantPoints: Use max() in the second pass.
Equal ratings need no extra candies.
commonMistakes: Overwriting instead of taking the maximum during the second pass.
Forgetting every child must receive at least one candy.
